column10: Q01Q01. How many years of professional software development experience do you have?
column11: G01Q05. What country are you based in?
column12: G01Q02[SQ002]. To what extent do you agree with the following sentences?  [I am familiar with the concept of microservice patterns]
column13: G01Q02[SQ003]. To what extent do you agree with the following sentences?  [I know how to fully develop microservice-based systems (design, debug, optimize)]
column14: G01Q02[SQ004]. To what extent do you agree with the following sentences?  [I have a solid understanding of this software repository]
column15: G01Q03. Approximately how many years have you used microservices in a software project?
column16: G01Q04[SQ001]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Single service instance per host                   SINGLE SERVICE INSTANCE PER HOST     Problem: How are services packaged and deployed?     Solution: Deploy each single service instance on its own host.    ]
column17: G01Q04[SQ002]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Multiple service instances per host                   MULTIPLE SERVICE INSTANCES PER HOST     Problem: How are services packaged and deployed?     Solution: Run multiple instances of different services on a host (Physical or Virtual machine).    ]
column18: G01Q04[SQ003]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Service instance per container                   SERVICE INSTANCE PER CONTAINER     Problem: How are services packaged and deployed?     Solution: Package the service as a (Docker) container image and deploy each service instance as a container.    ]
column19: G01Q04[SQ004]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Service instance per VM                   SERVICE INSTANCE PER VM     Problem: How are services packaged and deployed?     Solution: Package the service as a virtual machine image and deploy each service instance as a separate VM.    ]
column20: G01Q04[SQ005]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Service Deployment Platform                   SERVICE DEPLOYMENT PLATFORM     Problem: How are services packaged and deployed?     Solution: Use a deployment platform, which is automated infrastructure for application deployment. It provides a service abstraction, which is a named, set of highly available (e.g. load balanced) service instances.    ]
column21: G01Q04[SQ006]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Service mesh                   SERVICE MESH     Problem: You must implement numerous cross-cutting concerns including Externalized configuration, Logging, Health checks, Metrics, and Distributed tracing.     Solution: Use a service mesh that mediates all communication in and out of each service.    ]
column22: G01Q04[SQ007]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Service registry                       SERVICE REGISTRY     Problem: How do clients of a service (in the case of Client-side discovery) and/or routers (in the case of Server-side discovery) know about the available instances of a service?     Solution: Implement a service registry, which is a database of services, their instances and their locations. Service instances are registered with the service registry on startup and deregistered on shutdown. Client of the service and/or routers query the service registry to find the available instances of a service.    ]
column23: G01Q04[SQ008]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Server-side service discovery                       SERVER-SIDE SERVICE DISCOVERY     Problem: How does the client of a service - the API gateway or another service - discover the location of a service instance?     Solution: When making a request to a service, the client makes a request via a router (a.k.a load balancer) that runs at a well known location. The router queries a service registry, which might be built into the router, and forwards the request to an available service instance.    ]
column24: G01Q04[SQ009]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    3rd party registration                       3RD PARTY REGISTRATION     Problem: How are service instances registered with and unregistered from the service registry?     Solution: A 3rd party registrar is responsible for registering and unregistering a service instance with the service registry. When the service instance starts up, the registrar registers the service instance with the service registry. When the service instance shuts downs, the registrar unregisters the service instance from the service registry.    ]
column25: G01Q04Copy[SQ007]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Serverless deployment                       SERVERLESS DEPLOYMENT     Problem: How are services packaged and deployed?     Solution: Use a deployment infrastructure that hides any concept of servers - physical or virtual hosts, or containers. The infrastructure takes your service’s code and runs it. You are charged for each request based on the resources consumed.    ]
column26: G01Q04Copy[SQ009]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Client-side service discovery                       CLIENT-SIDE SERVICE DISCOVERY     Problem: How does the client of a service - the API gateway or another service - discover the location of a service instance?     Solution: When making a request to a service, the client obtains the location of a service instance by querying a Service Registry, which knows the locations of all service instances.    ]
column27: G01Q04Copy[SQ012]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Self registration                       SELF REGISTRATION     Problem: How are service instances registered with and unregistered from the service registry?     Solution: You have applied either the Client-side Service Discovery pattern or the Server-side Service Discovery pattern. Service instances must be registered with the service registry on startup so that they can be discovered and unregistered on shutdown.    ]
column28: G01Q04Copy[SQ013]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    API gateway/Backends for frontends                        API GATEWAY/BACKENDS FOR FRONTENDS     Problem: How do the clients of a Microservices-based application access the individual services?     Solution: Implement an API gateway that is the single entry point for all clients. The API gateway handles requests in one of two ways. Some requests are simply proxied/routed to the appropriate service. It handles other requests by fanning out to multiple services. A variation of this pattern is the Backends for frontends pattern. It defines a separate API gateway for each kind of client.    ]
column29: G01Q04Copy[SQ015]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Circuit Breaker                        CIRCUIT BREAKER     Problem: How to prevent a network or service failure from cascading to other services?     Solution: A service client should invoke a remote service via a proxy that functions in a similar fashion to an electrical circuit breaker. When the number of consecutive failures crosses a threshold, the circuit breaker trips, and for the duration of a timeout period all attempts to invoke the remote service will fail immediately. After the timeout expires the circuit breaker allows a limited number of test requests to pass through. If those requests succeed the circuit breaker resumes normal operation. Otherwise, if there is a failure the timeout period begins again.    ]
column30: G01Q04Copy[SQ016]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Idempotent Consumer                       IDEMPOTENT CONSUMER     Problem: How does a message consumer handle duplicate messages correctly?     Solution: Make a consumer idempotent by having it record the IDs of processed messages in the database. When processing a message, a consumer can detect and discard duplicates by querying the database.    ]
column31: G01Q04Copy[SQ017]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Remote Procedure Invocation                       REMOTE PROCEDURE INVOCATION     Problem: How do services in a microservice architecture communicate?     Solution: Use RPI for inter-service communication. The client uses a request/reply-based protocol to make requests to a service.    ]
column32: G01Q04Copy[SQ018]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Messaging                       MESSAGING     Problem: How do services in a microservice architecture communicate?     Solution: Use asynchronous messaging for inter-service communication. Services communicating by exchanging messages over messaging channels.  There are several different styles of asynchronous communication: Request/response, Notifications, Request/asynchronous response, Publish/subscribe, and Publish/asynchronous response.     ]
column33: G01Q04Copy[SQ019]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Transactional outbox                       TRANSACTIONAL OUTBOX     Problem: How to atomically update the database and send messages to a message broker?     Solution: The solution is for the service that sends the message to first store the message in the database as part of the transaction that updates the business entities. A separate process then sends the messages to the message broker.    ]
column34: G01Q04Copy[SQ020]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Transactional log tailing                       TRANSACTIONAL LOG TAILING     Problem: How to publish messages/events into the outbox in the database to the message broker?     Solution: Tail the database transaction log and publish each message/event inserted into the outbox to the message broker.    ]
column35: G01Q04Copy[SQ021]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Polling publisher                       POLLING PUBLISHER     Problem:How to publish messages/events into the outbox in the database to the message broker?     Solution: Publish messages by polling the database’s outbox table.    ]
column36: G01Q04Copy[SQ022]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Access token                       ACCESS TOKEN     Problem:How to communicate the identity of the requestor to the services that handle the request?     Solution: The API Gateway authenticates the request and passes an access token (e.g. JSON Web Token) that securely identifies the requestor in each request to the services. A service can include the access token in requests it makes to other services.    ]
column37: G01Q04Copy[SQ023]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Service template                       Service template     Problem:How can a team quickly create and setup a maintainable code base for a production-ready service so they can start developing its business logic?     Solution: Create a source code template that a developer can copy in order to quickly start developing a new service. A template is a simple runnable service that implements the required build logic and cross cutting concerns along with sample application logic.    ]
column38: G01Q04Copy[SQ025]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Externalized configuration                       Externalized configuration     Problem:How to enable a service to run in multiple environments without modification?     Solution: Externalize all application configuration including the database credentials and network location. On startup, a service reads the configuration from an external source, e.g. OS environment variables, etc.    ]
column39: G01Q04Copy[SQ026]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Microservice chassis                       MICROSERVICE CHASSIS     Problem:How can a team quickly create and setup a maintainable code base for a production-ready service so they can start developing its business logic?     Solution: Create a microservice chassis framework that can be foundation for developing your microservices. The chassis implements:  - Reusable build logic that builds, and tests a service.       - Mechanisms that handle cross-cutting concerns. The chassis typically assembles and configures a collection of frameworks and libraries that implement this functionality.    ]
column40: G01Q04Copy[SQ027]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Distributed tracing                       DISTRIBUTED TRACING     Problem:How to understand the behavior of an application and troubleshoot problems?     Solution: Instrument services with code that:      - Assigns each external request a unique external request id     - Passes the external request id to all services that are involved in handling the request      - Includes the external request id in all log messages      - Records information (e.g. start time, end time) about the requests and operations performed when handling a external request in a centralized service           ]
column41: G01Q04Copy[SQ028]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Application metrics                       APPLICATION METRICS     Problem:How to understand the behavior of an application and troubleshoot problems?     Solution: Instrument a service to gather statistics about individual operations. Aggregate metrics in centralized metrics service, which provides reporting and alerting.    ]
column42: G01Q04Copy[SQ029]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Health check API                       HEALTH CHECK API     Problem:How to detect that a running service instance is unable to handle requests?     Solution: A service has an health check API endpoint (e.g. HTTP /health) that returns the health of the service. The API endpoint handler performs various checks, such as:  - the status of the connections to the infrastructure services used by the service instance  - the status of the host, e.g. disk space  - application specific logic  A monitoring service, service registry or load balancer periodically invokes the endpoint to check the health of the service instance.     ]
column43: G01Q04Copy[SQ030]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Log aggregation                       LOG AGGREGATION     Problem:How to understand the behavior of an application and troubleshoot problems?     Solution: Use a centralized logging service that aggregates logs from each service instance. The users can search and analyze the logs. They can configure alerts that are triggered when certain messages appear in the logs.    ]
column44: G01Q04Copy[SQ031]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Exception tracking                       EXCEPTION TRACKING     Problem:How to understand the behavior of an application and troubleshoot problems?     Solution: Report all exceptions to a centralized exception tracking service that aggregates and tracks exceptions and notifies developers.    ]
column45: G01Q04Copy[SQ032]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Log deployments and changes                       LOG DEPLOYMENTS AND CHANGES     Problem:How to understand the behavior of an application and troubleshoot problems?     Solution: Log every deployment and every change to the (production) environment.    ]
column46: G01Q04Copy[SQ033]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Audit logging                       AUDIT LOGGING     Problem:How to understand the behavior of users and the application and troubleshoot problems?     Solution: Record user activity in a database.    ]
column47: G01Q04CopyCopy[SQ033]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Audit logging                       AUDIT LOGGING     Problem:How to understand the behavior of users and the application and troubleshoot problems?     Solution: Record user activity in a database.    ]
column48: G01Q04CopyCopy[SQ034]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [    Server-side page fragment composition                       SERVER-SIDE PAGE FRAGMENT COMPOSITION     Problem:How to implement a UI screen or page that displays data from multiple services?     Solution: Each team develops a web application that generates the HTML fragment that implements the region of the page for their service. A UI team is responsible for developing the page templates that build pages by performing server-side aggregation (e.g. server-side include style mechanism) of the service-specific HTML fragments.    ]
column49: G01Q04CopyCopy[SQ035]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Client-side UI composition                      CLIENT-SIDE UI COMPOSITION     Problem:How to implement a UI screen or page that displays data from multiple services?     Solution: Each team develops a client-side UI component, such an AngularJS directive, that implements the region of the page/screen for their service. A UI team is responsible implementing the page skeletons that build pages/screens by composing multiple, service-specific UI components.    ]
column50: G01Q04CopyCopy[SQ036]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Service integration contract test                      SERVICE INTEGRATION CONTRACT TEST     Problem:How to easily test that a service provides an API that its clients expect?     Solution: A test suite for a service that is written by the developers of another service that consumes it. The test suite verifies that the service meets the consuming service’s expectations.    ]
column51: G01Q04CopyCopy[SQ037]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Service component test                      SERVICE COMPONENT TEST     Problem:How do you easily test a service?     Solution: A test suite that tests a service in isolation using test doubles for any services that it invokes.    ]
column52: G01Q04CopyCopy[SQ038]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Event sourcing                      EVENT SOURCING     Problem:How to atomically update the database and send messages to a message broker?     Solution: A good solution to this problem is to use event sourcing. Event sourcing persists the state of a business entity such an Order or a Customer as a sequence of state-changing events. Whenever the state of a business entity changes, a new event is appended to the list of events. Since saving an event is a single operation, it is inherently atomic. The application reconstructs an entity’s current state by replaying the events.    ]
column53: G01Q04CopyCopy[SQ039]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Saga                      SAGA     Problem:How to implement transactions that span services?     Solution: Implement each business transaction that spans multiple services as a saga. A saga is a sequence of local transactions. Each local transaction updates the database and publishes a message or event to trigger the next local transaction in the saga. If a local transaction fails because it violates a business rule then the saga executes a series of compensating transactions that undo the changes that were made by the preceding local transactions.    ]
column54: G01Q04CopyCopy[SQ040]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Domain event                      DOMAIN EVENT     Problem:How does a service publish an event when it updates its data?     Solution: Organize the business logic of a service as a collection of DDD aggregates that emit domain events when they created or updated. The service publishes these domain events so that they can be consumed by other services.    ]
column55: G01Q04CopyCopy[SQ041]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Database per service                      DATABASE PER SERVICE     Problem:What’s the database architecture in a microservices application?     Solution: Keep each microservice’s persistent data private to that service and accessible only via its API. A service’s transactions only involve its database.    ]
column56: G01Q04CopyCopy[SQ042]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Shared database                      SHARED DATABASE     Problem:What’s the database architecture in a microservices application?     Solution: Use a (single) database that is shared by multiple services. Each service freely accesses data owned by other services using local ACID transactions.    ]
column57: G01Q04CopyCopy[SQ043]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   API composition                      API COMPOSITION     Problem:How to implement queries in a microservice architecture?     Solution: Implement a query by defining an API Composer, which invoking the services that own the data and performs an in-memory join of the results.    ]
column58: G01Q04CopyCopy[SQ044]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Command Query Responsibility Segregation (CQRS)                      COMMAND QUERY RESPONSIBILITY SEGREGATION (CQRS)     Problem:How to implement a query that retrieves data from multiple services in a microservice architecture?     Solution: Define a view database, which is a read-only ‘replica’ that is designed specifically to support that query, or a group related queries. The application keeps the database up to date by subscribing to Domain events published by the service that own the data. The type of database and its schema are optimized for the query or queries. It’s often a NoSQL database, such as a document database or a key-value store.    ]
column59: G01Q04CopyCopy[SQ045]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Decompose by business capability                      DECOMPOSE BY BUSINESS CAPABILITY     Problem:How to decompose an application into services?     Solution: Define services corresponding to business capabilities. A business capability is a concept from business architecture modeling. It is something that a business does in order to generate value. A business capability often corresponds to a business object.    ]
column60: G01Q04CopyCopy[SQ046]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Decompose by subdomain                      DECOMPOSE BY SUBDOMAIN     Problem:How to decompose an application into services?     Solution: Define services corresponding to Domain-Driven Design (DDD) subdomains. DDD refers to the application’s problem space - the business - as the domain. A domain is consists of multiple subdomains. Each subdomain corresponds to a different part of the business.    ]
column61: G01Q04CopyCopy[SQ047]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Self-contained service                      SELF-CONTAINED SERVICE     Problem:How should a service collaborate with other services when handling a synchronous request?     Solution: Design a service so that it can respond to a synchronous request without waiting for the response from any other service.    ]
column62: G01Q04CopyCopy[SQ048]. Are these patterns present in your repository: {TOKEN:ATTRIBUTE_1}?      [   Service per team                      SERVICE PER TEAM     Problem:What’s the relationship between teams and services?     Solution: Each service is owned by a team, which has sole responsibility for making changes. Ideally each team has only one service. Each team is responsible for one or more business functions (e.g. business capabilities). A team owns (has sole responsibility for changing) a code base consisting of one or more modules. Its code base is sized so as to not exceed the cognitive capacity of team. The team deploys its code as one or more services. A team should have exactly one service unless there is a proven need to have multiple services.    ]
